highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.215: Manual Invocation for /verify and /code-review
highlights.summary: Claude Code v2.1.215 stops automatically running /verify and /code-review skills, giving you full control over when these commands execute. To trigger a code review or verification, simply type /verify or /code-review yourself in the chat. This change helps reduce unexpected token usage and keeps your workflow more predictable.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.214: Critical Permission and Security Fixes
highlights.summary: This release patches two important security bugs: a directory permission rule that incorrectly allowed writes to nested folders anywhere in the file tree, and a permission-check bypass specific to Windows PowerShell 5.1 sessions. If you use allow rules like Edit(src/**) or run Claude Code on Windows, upgrading to v2.1.214 immediately is strongly recommended.

highlights.title: Claude Code v2.1.212: /fork Creates Background Sessions, /subtask Replaces In-Session Subagents
highlights.summary: The /fork command has been redesigned to copy your current conversation into a new background session visible in 'claude agents', so you can branch work without interrupting your current session. The previous in-session subagent behavior of /fork is now available as /subtask. Also new: claude auto-mode reset lets you restore default auto-mode settings with a confirmation prompt.

highlights.title: Claude Code Sends 33k Tokens Before Reading Your Prompt vs. OpenCode's 7k
highlights.summary: A detailed analysis by Systima AI found that Claude Code consumes roughly 33,000 tokens in overhead before it even processes your first prompt, compared to about 7,000 for OpenCode — a nearly 5x difference. This has real cost implications if you are on a token-based billing plan, and the article breaks down exactly where those tokens go. Developers on tight budgets should be aware of this overhead when choosing or configuring their AI coding tools.

highlights.title: Setting Up a Spare Mac for Claude Code to Control: Step-by-Step Guide
highlights.summary: This guide walks you through configuring a spare Mac as a dedicated machine that Claude Code can control remotely, enabling autonomous agent workflows without risking your primary development environment. It covers the necessary permissions, remote access setup, and safety considerations for letting Claude interact with a real desktop. Great for developers who want to experiment with agentic Claude Code tasks in a sandboxed environment.

highlights.title: Claude Code: Anatomy of a Misfeature
highlights.summary: This opinion piece dissects a specific Claude Code behavior the author considers a design flaw, exploring how seemingly helpful automation can introduce friction or unexpected results in real developer workflows. It provides a thoughtful critical perspective that can help you anticipate edge cases and set better expectations when using Claude Code. Worth reading to understand not just how to use the tool, but where its current design choices may work against you.

highlights.title: Mastering Claude Code in 30 Minutes
highlights.summary: This focused YouTube tutorial promises to get you productive with Claude Code in just 30 minutes, making it ideal for developers who want a fast, no-fluff introduction. It likely covers core commands, project setup, and practical coding workflows you can apply immediately. A great starting point if you prefer video learning over documentation.

highlights.title: Show HN: OtoDock – Run Claude Code and Codex as a Team of Agents on Your Server
highlights.summary: OtoDock is an open-source project that lets you orchestrate Claude Code and OpenAI Codex together as a coordinated team of agents running on your own server or homelab VMs. This is useful for developers who want multi-agent pipelines without relying on cloud-hosted orchestration services. Check the GitHub repo for setup instructions if you want to experiment with self-hosted agentic coding workflows.

tips_of_the_day: Now that /verify and /code-review must be invoked manually in Claude Code v2.1.215, add them as a habit at the end of each coding session — type /code-review before committing to catch issues without wasting tokens on automatic checks you didn't ask for.

If you use directory-scoped allow rules like Edit(src/**), double-check your permissions after upgrading to v2.1.214 to confirm that only your intended directories are writable — the previous bug may have granted broader access than you realized.

To reduce token overhead costs, consider starting Claude Code sessions with a focused, detailed prompt rather than iterating with many short messages — since Claude Code loads significant context upfront, you get more value per session by front-loading your requirements clearly.
